burial.plot_notes: Churches Corners Cemetery, Wheatland Township Hillsdale County MI. Per Abel Fowler genealogy.

narrative_blocks.body: Harriet Louisa Gridley and Anson Fowler married at Ogden NY in 1831 and later migrated to Wheatland Township in Hillsdale County Michigan — among the first Hooker-descended families to settle west of New York. Four of their five children were born in Michigan; Anson is buried at Churches Corners Cemetery there.

narrative_blocks.header: Charter member of frontier Free-Will Baptist Church
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet was an early member of the First Free-Will Baptist Church of Wheatland Michigan, organized February 19, 1838 in Eli Eastman's home shortly after the Fowlers arrived. The church gathered at Church's Corners just a few miles from her farm; local accounts noted that before the congregation organized, 'there was more rifle-shooting done on Sunday than any other day.'

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at forty-five, managed her own land deals
narrative_blocks.body: When Anson died in 1855 Harriet was forty-five, with five surviving children. Hillsdale County records show her buying and selling acreage within a few miles of the original 1835 patent for the next two decades — alone or jointly with relatives. She remained financially comfortable through the 1870 and 1880 censuses, outlived Anson by forty-three years, and died in Wheatland Township in 1898.

sources.label: Descendants of Abel Fowler (Wix-hosted PDF)
Melissa Craven Fowler — Fowler family genealogist

burial.plot_notes: Churches Corners Cemetery, Wheatland Township Hillsdale County MI.

parents.father_research_notes: Abel Fowler of Churchville NY. Per EH 1909 entry 2242 and the published Fowler genealogy 'Descendants of Abel Fowler.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Eddy Fowler. Per EH 1909 entry 2242.

narrative_blocks.header: Origin of the name Anson remains uncertain
narrative_blocks.body: Anson was born in Fairfield Herkimer County NY in 1809 to Abel and Mary Eddy Fowler — but the Fowler line shows no documented earlier Anson. Genealogists have suggested the name may trace to Mary's possible Eddy or Ross matrilineal kin: a William Anson Eddy and an Anson Ross were both born around 1809, and three Ross sisters had married three Eddy brothers in that family network. Whether the name came from those collateral relatives or from a popular contemporary figure named Anson is unresolved.

narrative_blocks.header: Frontier child became Michigan land-patent pioneer
narrative_blocks.body: Anson was about seven when his parents moved the family west from Herkimer County NY to Genesee County NY around 1816. After marrying Harriet Louisa Gridley in Ogden NY in 1831, the couple bought land in Wheatland Township Hillsdale County Michigan from a federal land office in 1835 — second-wave New England migration into the Old Northwest. Three of their five surviving children were born in Wheatland on that patent.

sources.label: Melissa Craven Fowler — Fowler family genealogist

burial.plot_notes: Oak Grove Cemetery, St. Louis Gratiot County MI. Per Abel Fowler genealogy.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries his maternal grandmother Lucilla Kirtland Gridley's maiden surname as middle name.

narrative_blocks.body: Edward's middle name 'Kirtland' came from his maternal grandmother Lucilla Kirtland Gridley — the same surname his uncle John Kirtland Gridley had inherited as a middle name a generation earlier. The Kirtland surname passed through three Gridley-Fowler generations as a middle name without anyone in the family carrying it as a last name.

burial.plot_notes: Mountain Grove Cemetery and Mausoleum, Bridgeport CT — originally interred 1907 alongside son Frederick. Remains exhumed and cremated 1932; final disposition of ashes unrecorded. The stone bearing his name at Church's Corners Cemetery (CEM207) in Wheatland MI alongside his parents Anson and Harriet Fowler is a cenotaph — the family considered Church's Corners as a possible final resting place for the ashes but this was never confirmed. Per Descendants of Abel Fowler PDF (Melissa Craven Fowler).

career.organization: Professor O. S. Fowler (phrenologist and lecturer)
career.notes: Joined the noted phrenologist Orson Squire Fowler in spring 1857 in Cincinnati and traveled with him visiting principal cities of the United States and Canada. Recommended for the post by shorthand instructor Andrew J. Graham. Whether Frank and O. S. Fowler were related is undocumented despite the shared surname.

career.organization: Public schools of Springfield Illinois and vicinity
career.notes: Awarded a lifetime teaching diploma by the Illinois Department of Public Instruction after rigid examination by the state superintendent. Originally moved to Springfield intending to take up legislative reporting; appropriation failed and he accepted a tutoring position instead.

Constructed a novel windmill at the request of a country friend, then realized the device was also a propellor and could be used for vessel propulsion and steering. Secured patents and exhibited the invention in Chicago, Buffalo, and New York City; awarded a medal by the American Institute and a diploma by the Centennial Commission. The work brought him to Bridgeport CT, but he later discovered he had no pecuniary interest in the enterprise and abandoned the field.

Returned to shorthand work after the windmill enterprise collapsed; within three years built an extensive practice. Author of two works on shorthand and developer of a high-speed shorthand process. Connecticut's general stenographic law of 1884 was passed largely through his patrons' efforts; appointed to an official position with the application signed by nearly every attorney in the county. First in his region to produce a daily transcript on the typewriter; reported the 1864 patriotic speeches of Generals Sherman, Logan, and Sickles, President Andrew Johnson, Senator Wilkinson, and Governors Yates and Oglesby.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries his mother Harriet Louisa Gridley Fowler's maiden surname as middle name — the same Gridley line he later honored in 1898 by exhuming his great-grandmother Mary Hooker Gridley's lost grave.

cross_connections.display_label: Exhumed his great-grandmother Mary's lost Pompey grave in 1898.

narrative_blocks.header: Exhumed his lost ancestor's grave in 1898
narrative_blocks.body: In 1898 Frank Gridley Fowler of Bridgeport CT searched out the abandoned Pompey NY graveyard where his great-grandmother Mary Hooker Gridley had been buried in 1819. He located the grave, had her remains exhumed and reinterred, and left an account of the search — a piece of nineteenth-century descendant research that Edward Hooker preserved verbatim in his 1909 genealogy.

narrative_blocks.header: Middle name became a research mission
narrative_blocks.body: Frank's middle name 'Gridley' carried his mother Harriet Louisa's maiden surname — the Gridley line that descended from Mary Hooker Gridley. Sixty years after his birth he turned that inherited name into action by exhuming Mary Hooker's lost grave at Pompey, completing a kind of genealogical homecoming.

narrative_blocks.header: His own grave became a research mystery
narrative_blocks.body: Frank died in Bridgeport CT in 1907 and was interred at Mountain Grove Cemetery alongside his son Frederick. In 1932 his remains were exhumed and cremated, but the final disposition of his ashes was never recorded — some sources suggest they may have been returned to Church's Corners in Michigan, where the stone bearing his name with his parents is a cenotaph. The man who in 1898 recovered his great-grandmother Mary Hooker Gridley's lost grave became himself, three decades later, another set of unaccounted-for remains.

sources.label: Melissa Craven Fowler — Fowler family genealogist

naming_inspiration.description: Carries her maternal grandmother Lucilla Kirtland Gridley's first name.

narrative_blocks.header: Died fourteen months after her wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Lucilla Maria Fowler married Harvey McGee in February 1871 in Jackson Michigan. She died at her family home at Wheatland Township in April 1872 at age twenty-one — fourteen months after the wedding. Her grandmother Lucilla Kirtland Gridley, after whom she was named, had also died young at forty-five.

career.location: Kensington CT, Durham NY, Pompey NY, North Chili NY
career.notes: Farmed across multiple Connecticut and New York locations through his lifetime.

military_service.notes: Soldier of the War of 1812, stationed at Oswego NY — Lake Ontario port and frontline against British attack 1814.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors his great-great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his ancestor Reverend Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker Gridley was given the full name of his great-great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford. The naming honored a preacher five generations removed who had died in 1647 — a 1775 echo of the Puritan founding.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 soldier at Oswego frontier
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker Gridley served as a soldier at Oswego NY during the War of 1812. The Lake Ontario port was captured by the British in May 1814 and the fort burned. His specific role and fate during the engagement are not documented in EH 1909.

parents.father_research_notes: Daniel Kirtland of Durham NY (originally Saybrook CT). Per EH 1909 entry 1093.
parents.mother_research_notes: Lovisa Lord Kirtland of Durham NY. Per EH 1909 entry 1093.

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Gridley Sr. of Kensington CT. Per EH 1909 entry 409.
parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Brownson Gridley of Kensington CT. Per EH 1909 entry 409.

sources.label: Hartford Genealogy Wiki — Mary (Hooker) Gridley

cross_connections.display_label: Her great-grandson Frank exhumed her lost grave in 1898.

narrative_blocks.body: Mary Hooker Gridley died at Pompey NY in 1819 and was buried in a small graveyard that was later abandoned. In 1898 her great-great-grandson Frank Gridley Fowler of Bridgeport CT searched out the lost cemetery, located her grave, and had her remains exhumed and reinterred — leaving an account of the search.

burialCemetery.name: Churches Corners Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Churches Corners Cemetery, Wheatland Township, Hillsdale County, Michigan. Burial place of Anson Fowler (X02653, 1809-1855) and his wife Harriet Louisa Gridley Fowler (H02242, 1810-1898). The first major Hooker-line burial site in Michigan. Per Descendants of Abel Fowler PDF.
